section.title: Corrupt Priests and Blemished Offerings
section.sectionRationale: The second and longest disputation, aimed squarely at the priests. Having declared His love, God confronts the contempt shown Him in worship: blind, lame, and sick animals offered on His altar, and priests who despise His name. It builds to a solemn warning that the covenant with Levi, meant to bring life and peace, has been broken.
section.thesis: God is a great King and Father who is owed honor and reverence; when the priests give Him their leftovers and lead the people astray, they profane His name and forfeit the blessing of the covenant of Levi.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the polluted offerings, where cheap sacrifices expose a contempt no governor would tolerate; and the warning to the priests, where God contrasts the reverent covenant with Levi against priests who have turned aside and made many stumble.

units.label: The polluted offerings
units.note: cheap sacrifices despise a great King

verses.text: “A son {{kavod|honors}} his father, and a servant his master. But if I am a father, where is My honor? And if I am a master, where is your fear of Me?” says the LORD of Hosts to you priests who {{bazah|despise}} My name. “But you ask, ‘How have we despised Your name?’
By presenting {{gaal|defiled}} food on My altar. But you ask, ‘How have we defiled You?’ By saying that the table of the LORD is contemptible.

When you offer blind animals for sacrifice, is it not wrong? And when you present the lame and sick ones, is it not wrong? Try offering them to your governor! Would he be pleased with you or show you favor?” asks the LORD of Hosts.
“But ask now for God’s favor. Will He be gracious? Since this has come from your hands, will He show you favor?” asks the LORD of Hosts.

“Oh, that one of you would shut the temple doors, so that you would no longer kindle useless fires on My altar! I take no pleasure in you,” says the LORD of Hosts, “and I will accept no offering from your hands.

For My name will be {{gadol|great}} among the nations, from where the sun rises to where it sets. In every place, incense and pure offerings will be presented in My name, because My name will be great among the nations,” says the LORD of Hosts.
“But you profane it when you say, ‘The table of the Lord is defiled, and as for its fruit, its food is contemptible.’

You also say: ‘Oh, what a nuisance!’ And you turn up your nose at it,” says the LORD of Hosts. “You bring offerings that are stolen, lame, or sick! Should I accept these from your hands?” asks the LORD.

“But cursed is the deceiver who has an acceptable male in his flock and vows to give it, but sacrifices a defective animal to the Lord. For I am a great King,” says the LORD of Hosts, “and My name is to be feared among the nations.
“And now this decree is for you, O priests:

If you do not listen, and if you do not take it to heart to honor My name,” says the LORD of Hosts, “I will send a curse among you, and I will curse your blessings. Yes, I have already begun to curse them, because you are not taking it to heart.
Behold, I will rebuke your descendants, and I will spread dung on your faces, the waste from your feasts, and you will be carried off with it.
Then you will know that I have sent you this commandment so that My {{berit|covenant}} with Levi may continue,” says the LORD of Hosts.
“My covenant with him was one of life and peace, which I gave to him; it called for reverence, and he revered Me and stood in awe of My name.
True instruction was in his mouth, and nothing false was found on his lips. He walked with Me in peace and uprightness, and he turned many from iniquity.
For the lips of a priest should preserve knowledge, and people should seek instruction from his mouth, because he is the {{malak|messenger}} of the LORD of Hosts.
But you have departed from the way, and your instruction has caused many to stumble. You have violated the covenant of Levi,” says the LORD of Hosts.
“So I in turn have made you despised and humiliated before all the people, because you have not kept My ways, but have shown partiality in matters of the law.”

kavod.gloss: Honor, weight, glory. A son gives weight to his father; God asks where the weight and honor due Him has gone. The word pictures treating God as heavy and significant rather than cheap and negligible, the very thing the priests failed to do.

bazah.gloss: To despise, to hold in contempt, to treat as worthless. The priests despised the LORD's name by treating His altar as a place to unload their worst animals. It is the opposite of honor: reckoning God as of little account while claiming to serve Him.

gaal.gloss: To defile, pollute, or make ceremonially loathsome. By offering blemished sacrifices the priests polluted the altar itself. The word exposes that careless worship does not merely fall short; it actively defiles what is meant to be holy.

gadol.gloss: Great, large in stature and worth. God declares that His name will be great among the nations even while His own priests treat it as small. The contrast is pointed: the peoples will honor what the covenant people have cheapened.

berit.gloss: Covenant, a binding, oath-bound relationship. The covenant with Levi was one of life and peace, calling for reverent, faithful teaching. God appeals to this covenant to show how far the priests have fallen and to warn that they have violated it.

malak.gloss: Messenger, envoy, one sent to carry another's word; the same root as the name Malachi and the messenger of 3:1. The priest is called the messenger of the LORD of Hosts because his lips are to preserve and deliver God's instruction. To corrupt his teaching is to betray his office.

basic.context: Having declared His love, God turns to the priests, who were meant to lead worship. Instead of offering their best, they brought blind, lame, and sick animals to the altar, the leftovers they would never dare give the Persian governor. God says this contempt despises His name, and He warns that the priesthood, meant to bring life and peace, has broken its covenant.
basic.hermeneutics: Again the disputation form drives it: God accuses, the priests ask How, and God spells it out. The ceremonial rules about unblemished animals no longer bind Christians in the same way, but the heart principle stands forever: God is worthy of our best, not our leftovers. Careless, half-hearted worship still despises His name.
basic.originalLanguages: God asks where the kavod, honor or weight, due a father is, since the priests bazah, despise, His name and gaal, defile, His altar with poor offerings. He insists His name will be gadol, great, among the nations. The priest is the malak, messenger, of the LORD, bound by the berit, covenant, with Levi to teach truth, a covenant they have now broken.
basic.typology: The priest as messenger who should preserve knowledge points forward to Christ, the perfect Priest and Messenger whose lips never spoke falsehood and who truly turns many from sin. The demand for an unblemished sacrifice points to Christ, the Lamb without blemish, the only offering God fully accepts.
basic.christ: The priests offered defective animals; God required perfection. That requirement is met only in Jesus, the Lamb without blemish or spot, 1 Peter 1:19, and the great High Priest whose teaching is wholly true. Where Levi's sons made many stumble, Christ turns many to righteousness and offers Himself as the one acceptable sacrifice.
basic.god: God is both Father and Master, worthy of honor and reverence. He is a great King whose name will be feared among the nations, not a minor deity to be fobbed off with scraps. He is holy: careless worship offends Him. Yet even His warning aims to restore the priests to the life and peace of the covenant.
basic.crossReferences: First Peter calls Christ a lamb without blemish, the perfect offering these priests failed to bring. Romans 12 urges us to present ourselves as living sacrifices, holy and pleasing to God. Hebrews 7 presents Jesus as the great High Priest who never fails in His office.
basic.oneStory: The failure of the priesthood runs through the whole Old Testament and cries out for a faithful Priest. Malachi's rebuke prepares the way for Christ, the true Messenger and spotless Lamb, whose worship and sacrifice are everything Israel's priests could not give.

context.literary: The second disputation and the longest sustained section, moving from the polluted offerings of 1:6-14 to the direct decree against the priests in 2:1-9. It is framed by the theme of God's name: despised by the priests but destined to be great and feared among the nations, a tension that drives the whole rebuke.
context.historical: In the Persian period the priests administered temple worship on the community's behalf. Economic hardship tempted people to hold back their best animals, and the priests, rather than guarding the altar, accepted and even offered the defective ones. The reference to the governor reflects the Persian administration that would have expected proper tribute, sharpening the insult that God received worse than a human official would tolerate.
context.canonical: The passage draws on the sacrificial requirements of Leviticus 22, which forbade blemished offerings, and on the covenant with Levi described in Numbers 25 and Deuteronomy 33. Verse 11, My name will be great among the nations, reaches ahead to the worldwide worship of the New Testament, and the ideal of the priest as messenger anticipates Christ the faithful High Priest of Hebrews.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the contempt hidden in the priests' careless worship, to call them back to the reverent, truthful ministry of the covenant with Levi, and to warn that continued corruption would bring curse rather than blessing on their office.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific rules about unblemished animals belonged to the old sacrificial system and are not binding on Christians in that form. What abides is the principle they embody: God deserves our best and truest worship, careless worship despises His name, and those who teach His word are accountable to teach it faithfully.

debates.question: What is meant by My name will be great among the nations in verse 11?

views.label: A future prophecy of worldwide worship
views.case: It looks ahead to the gospel age when peoples everywhere will offer pure worship in God's name, in contrast to the corrupt worship of the present priests.

views.label: A present rebuke by contrast
views.case: Even now God's name is held in higher regard beyond Israel's borders than by His own priests, heightening the shame of their contempt.

debates.bearing: Either way the verse magnifies God's name over against the priests' contempt and points toward the pure, worldwide worship fulfilled in Christ, so worship offered carelessly is exposed as an affront to a great King.

study.originalLanguages: The rebuke turns on the honor a great King is owed. A son gives kavod, weight and honor, to his father, but the priests bazah, despise, God's name and gaal, defile, His altar with blind and lame offerings. God insists His name will be gadol, great, among the nations from sunrise to sunset, even while His priests cheapen it. He then appeals to the berit, the covenant with Levi, a covenant of life and peace that called for reverence and true instruction, because the priest is the malak, the messenger of the LORD of Hosts. By corrupting their teaching and showing partiality, they have violated that covenant and forfeited its blessing.

typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Lamb without blemish or spot, the one offering God fully accepts, 1 Peter 1:19 and Hebrews 9:14.

typology.type: The priest as faithful messenger who turns many from sin
typology.fulfillment: Christ the great High Priest whose lips held only truth and who turns many to righteousness, Hebrews 7:26 and John 8:46.

typology.type: God's name great among the nations
typology.fulfillment: The worldwide worship of the gospel age, pure offerings brought in every place through Christ, John 4:21-24 and Revelation 5:9.

study.christ: The priests brought God blind, lame, and sick animals; the law demanded offerings without blemish; and no human priesthood ever supplied what God truly required. Jesus fulfills both sides of this passage. He is the spotless Lamb, offered once for all, the only sacrifice God fully accepts. And He is the faithful Priest and Messenger, the reality behind the covenant with Levi, whose lips preserved perfect knowledge and who turns many from iniquity rather than causing them to stumble. The promise that God's name will be great among the nations is answered as the gospel gathers pure worshipers from every people, offering praise in the name of Christ.
study.god: God presents Himself as Father, Master, and great King, worthy of honor, reverence, and fear. He is not indifferent to how He is worshiped; careless, cheap offerings insult Him, and He would rather the temple doors be shut than receive worthless worship. He is holy and just, ready to curse the blessings of unfaithful priests. Yet His aim is restoration, that the covenant of life and peace with Levi might continue, and His name be honored to the ends of the earth.

crossReferences.note: The law requiring unblemished sacrifices, which the priests were flagrantly breaking by offering blind, lame, and sick animals.
Christ is the lamb without blemish or spot, the perfect offering that Israel's priests failed to bring.
Jesus is the holy, faithful High Priest, unlike the corrupt priests of Malachi's day, who offered Himself once for all.
Believers are to present themselves as living sacrifices, holy and pleasing to God, the wholehearted worship the priests withheld.

study.oneStory: The corruption of the priesthood is a wound that runs through Israel's whole story, and Malachi lances it near the end of the Old Testament. God will not accept cheap worship, and He will not leave His altar defiled forever. The rebuke prepares the way for the true Priest and spotless Lamb, Jesus, in whom pure worship is offered and God's name is made great among all the nations.

sermon.title: Malachi's Message On Service (Malachi's Modern Message Part 2)
sermon.why: The second message of Legge's expository series through Malachi, covering 1:6-14 within this larger disputation on the priests and their polluted offerings. It works through the contempt shown God's name, the demand for our best in worship, and the accountability of those who serve at the altar, an orthodox exposition that points to Christ the spotless Lamb.

quiz.q: What was the priests' central sin in this passage?

options.text: They despised God's name by offering blind, lame, and sick animals instead of their best.
options.note: Verses 6-8 name the contempt shown in cheap, blemished sacrifices.

options.text: They failed to hold enough sacrifices each day.
options.note: The problem was the quality and heart of the worship, not its frequency.

options.text: They allowed foreigners to enter the temple.
options.note: That is not the charge here; the issue is defiled offerings and false teaching.

options.text: They offered too many expensive animals.
options.note: The opposite: they withheld their best and brought defective animals.

quiz.q: Why does God mention offering such animals to the governor?

options.text: To show that they gave God worse than they would dare give a human official.
options.note: Verse 8 exposes the insult: no governor would accept such gifts, yet they offered them to God.

options.text: To suggest they should pay more taxes to Persia.
options.note: The governor is an illustration of insult, not a lesson about taxes.

options.text: Because the governor controlled the temple.
options.note: The point is comparison, not the governor's authority over worship.

options.text: To praise the governor's generosity.
options.note: The governor is used to shame them, not to be praised.

quiz.q: What was the covenant with Levi meant to be?

options.text: A covenant of life and peace, calling for reverence and true instruction.
options.note: Verses 5-6 describe Levi's reverence and truthful teaching that turned many from sin.

options.text: A guarantee of wealth for the priests.
options.note: It brought life and peace, but its heart was reverent, faithful ministry, not riches.

options.text: A promise that priests could never be judged.
options.note: The passage shows priests very much held to account for breaking it.

options.text: A covenant that no longer mattered to God.
options.note: God appeals to it precisely because it still matters and has been violated.

quiz.q: Why is the priest called the messenger of the LORD of Hosts?

options.text: Because his lips are to preserve knowledge and deliver God's true instruction to the people.
options.note: Verse 7 grounds the title in the priest's duty to teach God's word faithfully.

options.text: Because he carried physical letters between towns.
options.note: Messenger here means bearer of God's word, not a courier of mail.

options.text: Because he predicted the future like a prophet.
options.note: The role is teaching God's law, not primarily foretelling.

options.text: Because he ruled over the governor.
options.note: The priest served under God as His messenger, not over the civil ruler.

quiz.q: How does this passage point to Christ?

options.text: He is the spotless Lamb and the faithful Priest and Messenger the corrupt priests could never be.
options.note: Christ fulfills both the unblemished offering and the truthful priestly ministry.

options.text: He teaches that worship no longer matters.
options.note: The opposite: He makes pure worship possible and His name great among the nations.

options.text: He shows that any sacrifice is acceptable to God.
options.note: God requires the unblemished offering, met only in Christ.

options.text: He has no connection to the priesthood of Malachi.
options.note: He is the true High Priest and Lamb that this passage anticipates.

questions: The priests gave God their leftovers rather than their best. Where might you be offering God the scraps of your time, energy, or money rather than your first and best?
God said He would rather the temple doors be shut than receive worthless worship. What would it look like for your worship to be wholehearted rather than going through the motions?
The priests despised God's name while still performing religious duties. How do you guard against a heart that keeps up outward religion while quietly holding God cheap?
The priest was to be God's messenger, preserving true instruction. If you teach or influence others, how carefully are you handling God's word so that you do not cause anyone to stumble?
Christ is the spotless Lamb who gives God the perfect worship we cannot. How does resting in His offering free you to worship joyfully rather than anxiously?

translationNotes.note: How have we defiled You renders the sense of polluting God's altar; a BSB footnote notes the Septuagint reads defiled it, referring to the altar or table rather than God directly.
My name will be great among the nations may be read as a future prophecy of worldwide worship or as a present rebuke by contrast; both magnify God's name over the priests' contempt.
I will rebuke your descendants carries a BSB footnote noting the phrase may also be rendered I will blight your grain, a fitting threat against the produce the priests withheld from God.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Malachi 1:6-2:9. All 18 verses reproduced in order (1:6-14 and 2:1-9). No em dashes occur in this range. BSB footnotes at 1:7 (LXX defiled it) and 2:3 (Or I will blight your grain) are reflected in the translation notes.
